items.passage: Globalization is the growing interdependence of the world's economies, cultures, and societies, produced by the flow of goods, capital, people, and information across national borders. [■] The term names not one event but a steady tightening of the links that bind distant communities together. A decision reached in one country, such as a sudden shift in consumer taste, can register within hours in markets on the far side of the planet. This tightening rests on the steadily falling cost of moving both things and ideas over long distances. Cheaper shipping, faster travel, and, above all, instant communication have shrunk the practical distance between nations. [■] Managers can therefore split the manufacture of a single product among many countries, placing each task wherever it can be done best or most cheaply. [■] The finished parts are then gathered together and shipped to consumers around the world. These dispersed networks reach well beyond the factory floor. Because national economies are now woven so tightly together, prosperity can spread more widely than before, yet so can risk. [■] However, a financial panic or a sudden shortage in one region can travel through the whole system within days, so the very links that spread wealth can also carry crises far and wide.
paragraphs: Globalization is the growing interdependence of the world's economies, cultures, and societies, produced by the flow of goods, capital, people, and information across national borders. [■] The term names not one event but a steady tightening of the links that bind distant communities together. A decision reached in one country, such as a sudden shift in consumer taste, can register within hours in markets on the far side of the planet.

This tightening rests on the steadily falling cost of moving both things and ideas over long distances. Cheaper shipping, faster travel, and, above all, instant communication have shrunk the practical distance between nations. [■] Managers can therefore split the manufacture of a single product among many countries, placing each task wherever it can be done best or most cheaply. [■] The finished parts are then gathered together and shipped to consumers around the world.

These dispersed networks reach well beyond the factory floor. Because national economies are now woven so tightly together, prosperity can spread more widely than before, yet so can risk. [■] However, a financial panic or a sudden shortage in one region can travel through the whole system within days, so the very links that spread wealth can also carry crises far and wide.

questions.stem: The word "dispersed" in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to

options.A: scattered
options.B: crowded
options.C: temporary
options.D: hidden

questions.explanation: The "dispersed networks" are those in which one product's manufacture is split "among many countries," that is, spread out or scattered across places.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what has cheaper transport and instant communication made possible?

options.A: It has ended nearly all trade between neighboring nations.
options.B: It has forced every country to produce the very same goods.
options.C: It lets firms divide one product's manufacture among several countries.
options.D: It has lowered the cost of shipping to almost nothing.

questions.explanation: The passage states that managers can now "split the manufacture of a single product among many countries, placing each task wherever it can be done best or most cheaply."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about tightly connected economies?

options.A: They are now largely protected from one another's economic problems.
options.B: A serious problem in one region can quickly affect others.
options.C: They have stopped competing with one another for trade.
options.D: They no longer experience any economic growth at all.

questions.explanation: Because "a financial panic or a sudden shortage in one region can travel through the whole system within days," trouble in one place readily spreads to others.

questions.stem: Why does the author state that a decision reached in one country can register within hours in distant markets (paragraph 1)?

options.A: To warn readers against investing in any foreign markets.
options.B: To show that distant markets rarely affect one another.
options.C: To describe how consumer tastes change over a single day.
options.D: To illustrate how closely distant economies are now linked.

questions.explanation: The instant, worldwide effect of one local decision illustrates the "steady tightening of the links that bind distant communities together."

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For example, a single clothing brand may design a garment in one country, have it stitched in another, and sell it in dozens more, coordinating every step by instant electronic messaging.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1
options.B: Location 2
options.C: Location 3
options.D: Location 4

questions.explanation: The clothing example is a concrete case of managers who "split the manufacture of a single product among many countries," so it fits at Location 3, right after that claim.

items.passage: Industrial organization refers to the way a firm arranges labor, machinery, and management to turn raw materials into finished goods. In the earliest workshops, a single skilled artisan built each product entirely, shaping and fitting every part by hand. [■] This method produced fine work but slowly, and because no two hand-made parts were alike, a broken item could rarely be repaired with pieces from another. Such craft production gave way, in the early factory era, to a division of labor in which each worker repeated one narrow task. Output rose sharply, yet the arrangement had a serious flaw. [■] Workers still carried materials from station to station, so unfinished goods piled up and idle time was common as faster hands waited for slower ones. [■] The pace of the whole line was set by its weakest point. This weakness was finally overcome by the moving assembly line, which reversed the old pattern by bringing the work to the worker. A powered belt carried each product past stationary workers, and its steady speed forced every task into a fixed rhythm. [■] Interchangeable parts, cut to standard sizes, snapped into place without adjustment. However, this rigid efficiency made the line hard to alter, so retooling for a new model could halt production for weeks.
paragraphs: Industrial organization refers to the way a firm arranges labor, machinery, and management to turn raw materials into finished goods. In the earliest workshops, a single skilled artisan built each product entirely, shaping and fitting every part by hand. [■] This method produced fine work but slowly, and because no two hand-made parts were alike, a broken item could rarely be repaired with pieces from another.

Such craft production gave way, in the early factory era, to a division of labor in which each worker repeated one narrow task. Output rose sharply, yet the arrangement had a serious flaw. [■] Workers still carried materials from station to station, so unfinished goods piled up and idle time was common as faster hands waited for slower ones. [■] The pace of the whole line was set by its weakest point.

This weakness was finally overcome by the moving assembly line, which reversed the old pattern by bringing the work to the worker. A powered belt carried each product past stationary workers, and its steady speed forced every task into a fixed rhythm. [■] Interchangeable parts, cut to standard sizes, snapped into place without adjustment. However, this rigid efficiency made the line hard to alter, so retooling for a new model could halt production for weeks.

questions.stem: The word "flaw" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: delay
options.B: expense
options.C: defect
options.D: task

questions.explanation: The "serious flaw" is the shortcoming the paragraph goes on to describe, namely the idle time caused by moving materials, so it means a defect.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, what was a drawback of the earliest workshops?

options.A: Products were made quickly but tended to wear out fast.
options.B: Products were made slowly and their parts were not interchangeable.
options.C: Workshops employed hundreds of unskilled laborers.
options.D: Artisans refused to share their techniques with rivals.

questions.explanation: The passage says the craft method "produced fine work but slowly," and because "no two hand-made parts were alike," items could rarely be repaired with parts from another.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT

options.A: The moving assembly line let workers set their own pace.
options.B: In craft workshops a single artisan built an entire product.
options.C: The division of labor increased a factory's output.
options.D: Interchangeable parts fit together without adjustment.

questions.explanation: The passage says the belt's "steady speed forced every task into a fixed rhythm," so the line set the pace, not the workers.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about the division of labor?

options.A: It removed nearly all wasted time from production.
options.B: Its speed was limited by the line's slowest workers.
options.C: It required each worker to master several different tasks.
options.D: It lowered the quality of the goods being produced.

questions.explanation: Since "faster hands waited for slower ones" and "the pace of the whole line was set by its weakest point," the slowest workers limited overall speed.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For example, at one early automobile plant the time needed to build a single car fell from over twelve hours to about ninety minutes once the line began to move.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1
options.B: Location 2
options.C: Location 3
options.D: Location 4

questions.explanation: The example illustrates the speed of the moving assembly line whose "steady speed forced every task into a fixed rhythm," so it fits at Location 4, right after that point.

items.passage: Plate tectonics is the theory that Earth's rigid outer shell is broken into several large plates that drift slowly over a hotter, softer layer below. Early in the twentieth century, the scientist Alfred Wegener proposed that the continents had once formed a single mass and had since moved apart. [■] Yet most geologists rejected the idea, because Wegener could name no force strong enough to push whole continents through the solid ocean floor. This missing mechanism was supplied decades later by studies of the deep ocean. Surveys revealed long undersea mountain ridges where molten rock rises, hardens, and spreads outward, widening the seabed on either side. [■] The rock on each flank carried matching magnetic stripes, mirror images proving that new floor forms symmetrically at the ridge. Continents, then, do not plow through the ocean floor; they ride slowly on top of it, like cargo on a conveyor belt. [■] This spreading seafloor tied together many scattered observations. Earthquakes, mountain belts, and the ranges of certain fossils all made sense once geologists mapped the plate boundaries where the moving slabs meet. [■] However, the theory still cannot predict exactly when the strain stored along these boundaries will break as an earthquake, so forecasting remains imprecise.
paragraphs: Plate tectonics is the theory that Earth's rigid outer shell is broken into several large plates that drift slowly over a hotter, softer layer below. Early in the twentieth century, the scientist Alfred Wegener proposed that the continents had once formed a single mass and had since moved apart. [■] Yet most geologists rejected the idea, because Wegener could name no force strong enough to push whole continents through the solid ocean floor.

This missing mechanism was supplied decades later by studies of the deep ocean. Surveys revealed long undersea mountain ridges where molten rock rises, hardens, and spreads outward, widening the seabed on either side. [■] The rock on each flank carried matching magnetic stripes, mirror images proving that new floor forms symmetrically at the ridge. Continents, then, do not plow through the ocean floor; they ride slowly on top of it, like cargo on a conveyor belt. [■]

This spreading seafloor tied together many scattered observations. Earthquakes, mountain belts, and the ranges of certain fossils all made sense once geologists mapped the plate boundaries where the moving slabs meet. [■] However, the theory still cannot predict exactly when the strain stored along these boundaries will break as an earthquake, so forecasting remains imprecise.

questions.stem: The word "rigid" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: stiff
options.B: thin
options.C: molten
options.D: ancient

questions.explanation: The "rigid outer shell" is contrasted with the "hotter, softer layer below," so rigid means hard and stiff rather than soft.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, why did most geologists reject Wegener's idea?

options.A: He claimed the continents had never once been joined together.
options.B: He had studied only a few small islands.
options.C: His measurements of the continents were inaccurate.
options.D: He could not identify a force able to move continents.

questions.explanation: The passage states that "Wegener could name no force strong enough to push whole continents through the solid ocean floor."

questions.stem: What does "This missing mechanism" refer to in paragraph 2?

options.A: the undersea mountain ridges
options.B: a force able to move the continents
options.C: the single early mass of the continents
options.D: the magnetic stripes in the rock

questions.explanation: The phrase points back to the force "strong enough to push whole continents" that Wegener could not name in paragraph 1.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about the ocean floor?

options.A: It has stayed unchanged since Earth first formed.
options.B: It is continually renewed rather than permanent.
options.C: It is younger near the continents than at the ridges.
options.D: It is made of exactly the same rock as the continents.

questions.explanation: Since molten rock "rises, hardens, and spreads outward," forming "new floor" at the ridge, the seabed is constantly created rather than fixed.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'Carried in this way, South America and Africa still creep apart today at about the rate a human fingernail grows, only a few centimeters a year.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1
options.B: Location 2
options.C: Location 3
options.D: Location 4

questions.explanation: The phrase "Carried in this way" refers back to continents riding "like cargo on a conveyor belt," so the sentence fits at Location 3, right after that image.

items.passage: Memory is the set of processes by which the brain encodes, stores, and later recovers information about past experience. Early researchers pictured it as an archive: an experience, once recorded, was thought to lie unchanged until needed, much as a book waits on a shelf. On this view, forgetting was merely a failure to find an intact record, not a change to it. This archival picture began to weaken as experiments revealed that remembering alters what is remembered. When people recalled an event, later questioning could reshape their account, folding in details that had only been suggested. The returning memory was not a clean copy but a version colored by everything learned since. Still, why the trace should change during recall remained unclear. This puzzle found its modern answer in a process called reconsolidation, in which a reactivated memory becomes briefly unstable and must be stored again. During this window the trace can be strengthened, weakened, or subtly rewritten before it settles. This casts memory less as a recording than as a living reconstruction, rebuilt slightly differently each use. However, because each retrieval can edit the trace, a vivid, confident memory is no guarantee of accuracy, a caution that courts weighing eyewitness testimony are only now absorbing.
paragraphs: Memory is the set of processes by which the brain encodes, stores, and later recovers information about past experience. Early researchers pictured it as an archive: an experience, once recorded, was thought to lie unchanged until needed, much as a book waits on a shelf. On this view, forgetting was merely a failure to find an intact record, not a change to it.

This archival picture began to weaken as experiments revealed that remembering alters what is remembered. When people recalled an event, later questioning could reshape their account, folding in details that had only been suggested. The returning memory was not a clean copy but a version colored by everything learned since. Still, why the trace should change during recall remained unclear.

This puzzle found its modern answer in a process called reconsolidation, in which a reactivated memory becomes briefly unstable and must be stored again. During this window the trace can be strengthened, weakened, or subtly rewritten before it settles. This casts memory less as a recording than as a living reconstruction, rebuilt slightly differently each use. However, because each retrieval can edit the trace, a vivid, confident memory is no guarantee of accuracy, a caution that courts weighing eyewitness testimony are only now absorbing.

questions.stem: The word "colored" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: darkened
options.B: influenced
options.C: recorded
options.D: shortened

questions.explanation: A memory "colored by everything learned since" is one shaped or influenced by later knowledge, not a clean copy.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what did experiments show about recalling an event?

options.A: Recalling an event permanently erased the original memory.
options.B: People recalled recent events far more accurately than old ones.
options.C: Repeated recall made a memory steadily more vivid.
options.D: Later questioning could add suggested details to a memory.

questions.explanation: The passage says "later questioning could reshape their account, folding in details that had only been suggested."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about a vivid, confident memory?

options.A: It is more likely than a faint memory to be correct.
options.B: It is usually formed during moments of strong emotion.
options.C: It may still be inaccurate despite feeling certain.
options.D: It cannot be altered once it has been stored.

questions.explanation: Because each retrieval can edit the trace, "a vivid, confident memory is no guarantee of accuracy," so strong confidence does not ensure correctness.

questions.stem: Why does the author compare an early memory to a book waiting on a shelf in paragraph 1?

options.A: To illustrate the early idea that stored memories stay unchanged.
options.B: To argue that memories are easily lost like misplaced books.
options.C: To explain how the brain physically stores information.
options.D: To describe the reading habits of early researchers.

questions.explanation: The book "waits on a shelf" until needed, illustrating the archival view that a recorded experience would "lie unchanged until needed."

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT

options.A: Early researchers saw memory as an unchanging record.
options.B: Recalling an event can alter what is remembered.
options.C: A reactivated memory becomes briefly unstable.
options.D: Reconsolidation makes a memory permanently fixed.

questions.explanation: Reconsolidation leaves a memory that "can be strengthened, weakened, or subtly rewritten," the opposite of permanently fixed, so this statement is not supported.

items.passage: Organic compounds are substances built around frameworks of carbon atoms, and many of them occur in two forms that are mirror images, like a left and right hand. The two versions, called enantiomers, share identical bonds and formulas, yet they are not interchangeable. In living systems this difference is decisive: one form of a drug may heal, while its mirror image does nothing or even harms. A synthesis making both forms equally therefore wastes half its product and can carry real danger. This danger drove chemists to find ways to build just one mirror form while excluding the other. They developed special catalysts, themselves handed, that steer a reaction so incoming atoms can attach in only one orientation. Because a catalyst is not consumed, a small amount can direct the assembly of vast quantities of product, making the selective route practical on an industrial scale. This approach transformed the making of medicines, letting manufacturers produce the active form of a drug cleanly and discard little. It also lowered the risk of contaminating a remedy with a harmful twin. However, designing a catalyst precise enough for a given target remains slow and largely a matter of trial, so for many valuable compounds no efficient single-form route has yet been found.
paragraphs: Organic compounds are substances built around frameworks of carbon atoms, and many of them occur in two forms that are mirror images, like a left and right hand. The two versions, called enantiomers, share identical bonds and formulas, yet they are not interchangeable. In living systems this difference is decisive: one form of a drug may heal, while its mirror image does nothing or even harms. A synthesis making both forms equally therefore wastes half its product and can carry real danger.

This danger drove chemists to find ways to build just one mirror form while excluding the other. They developed special catalysts, themselves handed, that steer a reaction so incoming atoms can attach in only one orientation. Because a catalyst is not consumed, a small amount can direct the assembly of vast quantities of product, making the selective route practical on an industrial scale.

This approach transformed the making of medicines, letting manufacturers produce the active form of a drug cleanly and discard little. It also lowered the risk of contaminating a remedy with a harmful twin. However, designing a catalyst precise enough for a given target remains slow and largely a matter of trial, so for many valuable compounds no efficient single-form route has yet been found.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: How industrial catalysts are recovered and reused after reactions.
options.B: Why one form of a drug molecule can harm a patient.
options.C: The problem of mirror-image molecules and one way to solve it.
options.D: How carbon atoms link together to form long chains and rings.

questions.explanation: The passage sets out the difficulty that enantiomers create and then describes handed catalysts as a method for building just one form, covering both problem and solution.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, why is a synthesis that yields both enantiomers a problem?

options.A: Half the product is wasted and one form may be harmful.
options.B: The two forms react with one another and destroy each other.
options.C: Both forms are too unstable to be stored safely.
options.D: The resulting mixture cannot be dissolved in water.

questions.explanation: The passage says such a synthesis "wastes half its product and can carry real danger," since one mirror form "does nothing or even harms."

questions.stem: How does paragraph 2 relate to paragraph 1?

options.A: It restates paragraph 1's problem using more technical terms.
options.B: It presents a solution to the difficulty described in paragraph 1.
options.C: It gives evidence that contradicts paragraph 1's claims.
options.D: It lists several exceptions to the rule stated in paragraph 1.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 opens by describing how chemists "find ways to build just one mirror form," directly answering the wasteful, dangerous synthesis of paragraph 1.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about the handed catalysts?

options.A: They must be replaced after each new batch of product.
options.B: A small quantity can produce a large amount of product.
options.C: They work only at very high temperatures.
options.D: They combine permanently with the finished product.

questions.explanation: Because a catalyst "is not consumed, a small amount can direct the assembly of vast quantities of product," so little catalyst yields much product.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, what limitation of the selective approach still remains?

options.A: The active form of a drug still cannot be separated cleanly enough.
options.B: The catalysts are too expensive for most manufacturers to buy.
options.C: The harmful mirror form can no longer be removed at all.
options.D: Designing a precise catalyst is slow and often a matter of trial.

questions.explanation: The passage states that "designing a catalyst precise enough for a given target remains slow and largely a matter of trial."
